game_name: Might and Magic III: Isles of Terra

danger_signals.visual_form: Creatures appear and become visible as player approaches from multiple squares away
danger_signals.meaning: Advance warning of hostile encounters; player can prepare tactics or attempt evasion

danger_signals.visual_form: Character faces in HUD display different expressions based on health/status
danger_signals.meaning: Visual indicator of party vulnerability; damage, poison, curses, or weakness states communicated through facial animation

danger_signals.visual_form: Arena and Castle Greywind Dungeon environments lack save functionality
danger_signals.meaning: High-danger locations where progress is permanent and failure carries severe consequences

danger_signals.visual_form: Narrative manifestation of villain launching nacelles into suns, destroying worlds
danger_signals.meaning: World-state threat; escalating danger to all realms on Isles of Terra

safety_signals.visual_form: Town hub spaces with inns (e.g., Fountain Head starting town)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone for rest and resource recovery; prior games required inn visits for saving
Player agency and safety; reduces permanent consequence threat compared to prior games

safety_signals.visual_form: NPC ally who provides journals and instructions throughout journey
safety_signals.meaning: Ally faction; source of strategic knowledge and support against Sheltem

safety_signals.visual_form: Safe settlement areas with merchant and quest-giver presence
safety_signals.meaning: Civilized zones; access to commerce and social structure

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Pyramids of the Ancients; underwater seedship 'Lincoln'
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Ancient ruins, control centers, mysterious dome structures; oceanic/submerged architecture

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Escape pod; nacelle worlds being thrust into suns
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Enemy creatures and forces aligned with Sheltem's machinations
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Corrupted nacelle worlds; areas of destruction and world-ending threats
Elemental-themed dungeons and zones (implied by narrative)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: 'Ultimate Adventurers' title earned from Maze from Hell
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Character face representations in HUD; party-specific equipment and spells

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Nacelles visible in sky or world geometry; suns showing danger or distortion
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased enemy creature encounters; environmental hazards escalate; NPCs express urgency in dialogue

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character faces display healthier expressions; improved equipment visible on character portraits
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies encountered become more varied and dangerous; access to previously restricted areas

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Colorful VGA graphics showing distinct island environments; sea/ocean surroundings emphasize island isolation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Automapping reveals new territories; creature populations vary by region; environmental storytelling through architecture

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Approach to end-game (Maze from Hell / Control Center)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Increasingly abstract and alien architectural styles; underwater seedship aesthetics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies reach maximum difficulty; maze defenses present unique challenges; final confrontation staging

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Cleared dungeon zones remain empty when revisited (unlike prior games)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Persistent world changes; player choices have lasting environmental impact

environmental_storytelling.example: Fountain Head starting town uses distinct visual style; later zones employ elemental or ancient aesthetic to signal faction/danger
Isles of Terra structured as separate 'nacelle' worlds, visually communicated through distinct geographical regions and travel mechanics
Populated towns signal safety and commerce; dungeon solitude signals danger; Corak's journals provide lore exposition
Increasing complexity and visual alienation as player descends toward final Control Center; Maze from Hell represents ultimate environmental challenge
Different creature types inhabit different regions, communicating local danger and thematic environmental identity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Character face expressions appear neutral or confident; normal movement and combat speed
Character face displays pain or distress expression; reduced combat effectiveness implied by visual HUD
Facial expressions show affliction; status conditions visually communicated through character portrait changes
Creatures visible at distance but stationary; no aggressive approach
Creatures become visible as distance closes; approach party positions; enter turn-based combat
Loot appears automatically in inventory; creatures no longer respawn in cleared zones (mechanically distinct from prior games)
Narrative reveals escalating actions; launching nacelles into suns; piloting escape pod toward Xeen; fleeing from Corak
Provides journal entries; communicates mission objectives; pursues Sheltem; instructs final matter transferral sequence

notes: Might and Magic III represents a significant visual upgrade from prior entries, leveraging enhanced graphics technology to communicate world-state more effectively. The HUD redesign replacing text summaries with character portraits that express emotional/health states is a key manifestation layer innovation. The game's distinction between save-enabled and save-restricted zones creates implicit danger signals through game mechanics reflected in environment design. The first-person isometric perspective allows distance-based enemy visibility, fundamentally changing how danger signals communicate to the player. The archipelago setting (Isles of Terra as separate nacelle worlds) is communicated through distinct visual regions, reinforcing narrative faction separation. Sheltem's antagonistic faction is primarily communicated through narrative consequences (world destruction) rather than direct visual faction markers, suggesting an implicit 'environmental corruption' aesthetic tied to his presence.
